words.shortDefinition: A statement that a certain condition is true in a test.
words.simpleDefinition: A logical check in a test declaring what the correct outcome should be — failing the entire test if the actual result differs from the expected state. Assertions act as the binary pass/fail logic of automation.
words.longDefinition: An assertion is a boolean expression at a specific point in a program which will be true unless there is a bug; in testing, it's used to compare the actual result with the expected result.
words.connector: Think of an assertion like a referee's call — you state the rule, and if reality disagrees, you blow the whistle immediately.
words.techContext: Every test case must have at least one assertion to determine whether the test passed or failed.
words.exampleSentence: The test failed because the assertion expected the status code to be 200, but it received 500.
words.etymology: From Latin 'asserere', to claim or maintain.

codeExample.code: assert.strictEqual(calculateTotal(10, 5), 15);

words.shortDefinition: A collection of related test cases.
words.simpleDefinition: A logical grouping of related test cases executed together — providing a high-level summary of a specific feature's health. Suites allow developers to organize thousands of individual tests into manageable, searchable, and runnable batches.
words.longDefinition: A test suite is a container that has a set of tests which help testers in executing and reporting the test execution status.
words.connector: Think of a test suite like a car's safety inspection — multiple separate checks run together to give one overall pass/fail status.
words.techContext: We grouped all our login-related tests into a single test suite to make it easier to run them together.
words.exampleSentence: The full test suite takes about ten minutes to run in our CI/CD pipeline.

words.shortDefinition: A three-part scenario template that structures a behavior test by precondition, action, and expected outcome.
words.simpleDefinition: A test pattern: state the setup (Given), the trigger (When), then assert the result (Then).
words.longDefinition: Given/When/Then is the BDD scenario shape — readable enough that PMs can review it, structured enough that frameworks like Cucumber or Gherkin can parse it. The trio maps cleanly onto the AAA pattern (Arrange/Act/Assert) used in xUnit-style tests, but reads as English.
words.techContext: Cucumber, SpecFlow, JBehave, and Behave all consume Gherkin Given/When/Then files. Even outside BDD frameworks, the structure makes hand-written unit tests narrate what they actually test.
words.exampleSentence: Write the new auth flow as Given/When/Then scenarios so QA and product can sign off on the same artifact.
words.etymology: From Behavior-Driven Development (BDD), formalized by Dan North in the early 2000s.
words.connector: Like a recipe — list the ingredients, do the steps, taste the result.

codeExample.code:
Scenario: Login with valid credentials
  Given a registered user with email "ada@example.com"
  When the user submits the correct password
  Then they are redirected to the dashboard
  And a session cookie is set

words.shortDefinition: A tool that executes tests and reports the results.
words.simpleDefinition: Software utility that discovers and executes test files automatically — aggregating results into a readable report. It manages the execution environment, handles timeouts, and provides the command-line interface for running the test suite.
words.longDefinition: A test runner is a software tool that automatically picks up and executes test files, provides a dashboard for the results, and can often watch files for changes.
words.connector: Think of a test runner like an exam proctor — they hand out the tests, time the session, and collect all the marks.
words.techContext: Jest is a popular test runner for JavaScript that provides a fast and interactive testing experience.
words.exampleSentence: The test runner highlighted the failing tests in red, making it easy to see where the regression occurred.

codeExample.code:
$ npm test
# This command invokes the test runner

words.shortDefinition: Testing where the internal structure of the item is unknown to the tester.
words.simpleDefinition: Testing where the internal code or logic is unknown to the tester — focusing entirely on inputs and outputs from the user's perspective. It validates that the system fulfills requirements without being biased by implementation details.
words.longDefinition: Black-box testing focuses on the inputs and outputs of the software without any knowledge of the internal code or logic.
words.connector: Think of black-box testing like using a vending machine — you press buttons and check the output without knowing the internal wiring.
words.techContext: Black-box testing is often performed by a dedicated QA team who only has access to the user interface and requirements.
words.exampleSentence: In black-box testing, we only care that the login works, not how the authentication function is implemented.
words.etymology: Metaphor for a 'black box' where you cannot see what is inside.

words.shortDefinition: Automated testing to verify that new code changes haven't broken existing system functionality.
words.simpleDefinition: The automated process of re-running old tests to confirm that new code hasn't accidentally broken existing features. It acts as a safety net — preventing the software from 'regressing' into a buggier state during rapid development cycles.
words.longDefinition: Regression testing involves executing a suite of functional tests whenever code is updated to ensure that previously working features remain intact. In a CI/CD environment, this usually consists of unit, integration, and end-to-end tests that run on every pull request. By validating that new features don't introduce side effects in unrelated modules, regression testing gives teams the confidence to move fast and release software frequently without fear of breakage.
words.connector: Think of regression testing like a health checkup after surgery — you're ensuring the fix didn't cause new problems elsewhere in the body.
words.techContext: Our regression testing suite caught a bug in the checkout flow that was caused by a small change in the tax calculation library.
words.exampleSentence: The regression testing suite caught a critical failure in the login flow that was caused by a minor change in the cookie-parsing logic.

words.shortDefinition: Preliminary testing to reveal simple failures severe enough to reject a prospective release.
words.simpleDefinition: A rapid, preliminary test pass identifying critical failures severe enough to reject a release immediately — verifying basic stability like 'does the app start?'. Acts as a gatekeeper before deeper testing begins.
words.longDefinition: Smoke testing is a quick, high-level test run to ensure the most critical functions of an application work (e.g., does it even start?) before proceeding with more detailed testing.
words.connector: Think of smoke testing like turning on a toaster for the first time — if smoke comes out, you stop immediately; otherwise, you continue.
words.techContext: If the smoke testing fails, we immediately reject the build and send it back to the developers to save time.
words.exampleSentence: The smoke testing pass confirmed that the core dashboard and login are working after the server migration.
words.etymology: Metaphor from plumbing/electronics, where smoke is used to find obvious leaks or shorts.

words.shortDefinition: Quick testing to verify that a specific bug fix works as expected.
words.simpleDefinition: Narrow, highly-focused check performed after a specific bug fix to ensure that only the relevant functionality works as intended. Unlike regression testing which is broad, sanity testing is limited to the specific area touched.
words.longDefinition: Sanity testing is a subset of regression testing used to verify that a specific feature or bug fix works as intended after a change, without running the entire suite.
words.connector: Think of sanity testing like checking your pulse after a run — it is a quick check to see if you are okay before doing a full exam.
words.techContext: After the developer fixed the logout issue, I performed a sanity testing check to make sure the fix worked before starting the full regression suite.
words.exampleSentence: Sanity testing showed that the spelling error on the landing page was corrected.
words.etymology: From Latin 'sanitas', health or soundness of mind.

words.shortDefinition: Testing where the internal structure of the item is known to the tester.
words.simpleDefinition: Testing where the internal source code is visible and known to the tester — allowing them to design tests that exercise specific internal logic paths, branches, and conditions. Typically performed by developers through unit and integration tests.
words.longDefinition: White-box testing (also known as clear-box testing) is a method where the tester has access to the internal code and can design tests based on the logic and paths within the software.
words.connector: Think of white-box testing like a mechanic checking an engine with the hood open — they see exactly how everything connects and moves.
words.techContext: Developers use white-box testing to ensure that all branches of an 'if/else' statement are correctly exercised by the test suite.
words.exampleSentence: White-box testing is useful for finding hidden logic errors that might not be apparent from the UI alone.
words.etymology: Metaphor for a 'white' or 'clear' box where you can see the internal contents.
